question:
A-6. Given real numbers \( \left\{a_{i}\right\} \) and \( \left\{b_{i}\right\},(i=1,2,3,4) \), such that \( a_{1} b_{2}-a_{2} b_{1} \neq 0 \). Consider the set of all solutions ( \( x_{1}, x_{2}, x_{8}, x_{4} \) ) of the simultaneous equations
\[
a_{1} x_{1}+a_{2} x_{2}+a_{2} x_{3}+a_{4} x_{4}=0 \text { and } b_{1} x_{1}+b_{2} x_{2}+b_{4} x_{3}+b_{4} x_{4}=0
\]
for which no \( x_{i}(i=1,2,3,4) \) is zero. Each such solution generates a 4-tuple of plus and minus signs (signum \( x_{1} \), signum \( x_{2} \), signum \( x_{3} \), signum \( x_{4} \) ).
(a) Determine, with a proof, the maximum number of distinct 4-tuples possible.
(b) Investigate necessary and sufficient conditions on the real numbers \( \left\{a_{i}\right\} \) and \( \left\{b_{i}\right\} \) such that the above maximum number of 4 -tuples is attained.

descriptive_long.question:
A-6. Given real numbers \( \left\{a_{i}\right\} \) and \( \left\{b_{i}\right\},(i=1,2,3,4) \), such that \( acoeffone bcoefftwo-acoefftwo bcoeffone \neq 0 \). Consider the set of all solutions ( \( firstcoord, secondcoord, x_{8}, fourthcoord \) ) of the simultaneous equations
\[
acoeffone firstcoord+acoefftwo secondcoord+acoefftwo thirdcoord+acoefffour fourthcoord=0 \text { and } bcoeffone firstcoord+bcoefftwo secondcoord+bcoefffour thirdcoord+bcoefffour fourthcoord=0
\]
for which no \( x_{i}(i=1,2,3,4) \) is zero. Each such solution generates a 4-tuple of plus and minus signs (signum \( firstcoord \), signum \( secondcoord \), signum \( thirdcoord \), signum \( fourthcoord \) ).
(a) Determine, with a proof, the maximum number of distinct 4-tuples possible.
(b) Investigate necessary and sufficient conditions on the real numbers \( \left\{a_{i}\right\} \) and \( \left\{b_{i}\right\} \) such that the above maximum number of 4 -tuples is attained.
descriptive_long.solution:
A-6 Solving the given equations in terms of thirdcoord and fourthcoord, leads to the equivalent system: \( firstcoord=matrixaone thirdcoord+matrixbone fourthcoord, \quad secondcoord=matrixatwo thirdcoord+matrixbtwo fourthcoord, \quad thirdcoord=thirdcoord, \quad fourthcoord=fourthcoord \), where \( matrixaone=(acoefftwo bcoeffthree-acoeffthree bcoefftwo)/(acoeffone bcoefftwo-acoefftwo bcoeffone) \), etc.

Each point in the thirdcoord, fourthcoord-plane corresponds uniquely to a solution ( firstcoord, secondcoord, thirdcoord, fourthcoord ). Signum firstcoord is positive for ( thirdcoord, fourthcoord ) on one side of \( matrixaone thirdcoord+matrixbone fourthcoord=0 \) and negative on the other side. Similarly for signum secondcoord, signum thirdcoord, and signum fourthcoord, using \( matrixatwo thirdcoord+matrixbtwo thirdcoord=0, thirdcoord=0 \) and \( fourthcoord=0 \), respectively. These four lines through the origin, in general, divide the plane into eight regions, each having a different 4 -tuple of signum values. Hence the maximum number of distinct 4 -tuples is eight.

The maximum number of eight will occur if and only if there are actually four distinct lines. This is equivalent to the conditions \( matrixaone 
eq 0, matrixatwo 
eq 0, matrixbone 
eq 0, matrixbtwo 
eq 0 \) and \( matrixaone matrixbtwo-matrixatwo matrixbone 
eq 0 \) or, simply, \( a_{i} b_{j}-a_{j} b_{i} 
eq 0 \) for \( i, j=1,2,3,4 \) and \( i<j \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
A-6. Given real numbers \( \left\{a_{i}\right\} \) and \( \left\{b_{i}\right\},(i=1,2,3,4) \), such that \( gemstone\,parchment-sailboat\,thunderclap \neq 0 \). Consider the set of all solutions ( \( sunflower, raincloud, x_{8}, paintbrush \) ) of the simultaneous equations
\[
gemstone\,sunflower+sailboat\,raincloud+sailboat\,bookshelf+driftwood\,paintbrush=0 \text { and } thunderclap\,sunflower+parchment\,raincloud+coppermine\,bookshelf+coppermine\,paintbrush=0
\]
for which no \( x_{i}(i=1,2,3,4) \) is zero. Each such solution generates a 4-tuple of plus and minus signs (signum \( sunflower \), signum \( raincloud \), signum \( bookshelf \), signum \( paintbrush \) ).
(a) Determine, with a proof, the maximum number of distinct 4-tuples possible.
(b) Investigate necessary and sufficient conditions on the real numbers \( \left\{a_{i}\right\} \) and \( \left\{b_{i}\right\} \) such that the above maximum number of 4 -tuples is attained.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
A-6 Solving the given equations in terms of \( bookshelf \) and \( paintbrush \), leads to the equivalent system: \( sunflower=moonbeams\,bookshelf+fireflies\,paintbrush, \quad raincloud=sandcastle\,bookshelf+periscope\,paintbrush, \quad bookshelf=bookshelf, \quad paintbrush=paintbrush \), where \( moonbeams=\left(sailboat\,waterfall-starlight\,parchmentight) /\left(gemstone\,parchment-sailboat\,thunderclapight) \), etc.

Each point in the \( bookshelf, paintbrush \)-plane corresponds uniquely to a solution ( \( sunflower, raincloud \), \( bookshelf, paintbrush \) ). Signum \( sunflower \) is positive for ( \( bookshelf, paintbrush \) ) on one side of \( moonbeams\,bookshelf+fireflies\,paintbrush=0 \) and negative on the other side. Similarly for signum \( raincloud \), signum \( bookshelf \), and signum \( paintbrush \), using \( sandcastle\,bookshelf+periscope\,bookshelf=0, bookshelf=0 \) and \( paintbrush=0 \), respectively. These four lines through the origin, in general, divide the plane into eight regions, each having a different 4 -tuple of signum values. Hence the maximum number of distinct 4 -tuples is eight.

The maximum number of eight will occur if and only if there are actually four distinct lines. This is equivalent to the conditions \( moonbeams 
eq 0, sandcastle 
eq 0, fireflies 
eq 0 \), \( periscope 
eq 0 \) and \( moonbeams\,periscope-sandcastle\,fireflies 
eq 0 \) or, simply, \( a_{i} b_{j}-a_{j} b_{i} 
eq 0 \) for \( i, j=1,2,3,4 \) and \( i<j \).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
A-6. Given real numbers \( \left\{a_{i}\right\} \) and \( \left\{b_{i}\right\},(i=1,2,3,4) \), such that \( variableone mutabletwo-variabletwo mutableone \neq 0 \). Consider the set of all solutions ( \( knownvalue, fixedvalue, x_{8}, specified \) ) of the simultaneous equations
\[
variableone knownvalue+variabletwo fixedvalue+variabletwo determined+variablefour specified=0 \text { and } mutableone knownvalue+mutabletwo fixedvalue+mutablefour determined+mutablefour specified=0
\]
for which no \( x_{i}(i=1,2,3,4) \) is zero. Each such solution generates a 4-tuple of plus and minus signs (signum \( knownvalue \), signum \( fixedvalue \), signum \( determined \), signum \( specified \) ).
(a) Determine, with a proof, the maximum number of distinct 4-tuples possible.
(b) Investigate necessary and sufficient conditions on the real numbers \( \left\{a_{i}\right\} \) and \( \left\{b_{i}\right\} \) such that the above maximum number of 4 -tuples is attained.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
A-6 Solving the given equations in terms of \( determined \) and \( specified \), leads to the equivalent system: \( knownvalue=horizontalone determined+verticalone specified, \quad fixedvalue=horizontaltwo determined+verticaltwo specified, \quad determined=determined, \quad specified=specified \), where \( horizontalone=\left(variabletwo mutablethree-variablethree mutabletwoight) /\left(variableone mutabletwo-variabletwo mutableoneight) \), etc.

Each point in the \( determined, specified \)-plane corresponds uniquely to a solution ( \( knownvalue, fixedvalue \), \( determined, specified \) ). Signum \( knownvalue \) is positive for ( \( determined, specified \) ) on one side of \( horizontalone determined+verticalone specified=0 \) and negative on the other side. Similarly for signum \( fixedvalue \), signum \( determined \), and signum \( specified \), using \( horizontaltwo determined+verticaltwo determined=0, determined=0 \) and \( specified=0 \), respectively. These four lines through the origin, in general, divide the plane into eight regions, each having a different 4 -tuple of signum values. Hence the maximum number of distinct 4 -tuples is eight.

The maximum number of eight will occur if and only if there are actually four distinct lines. This is equivalent to the conditions \( horizontalone 
eq 0, horizontaltwo 
eq 0, verticalone 
eq 0, verticaltwo 
eq 0 \) and \( horizontalone verticaltwo-horizontaltwo verticalone 
eq 0 \) or, simply, \( a_{i} b_{j}-a_{j} b_{i} 
eq 0 \) for \( i, j=1,2,3,4 \) and \( i<j \).

garbled_string.question:
A-6. Given real numbers \( \left\{a_{i}\right\} \) and \( \left\{b_{i}\right\},(i=1,2,3,4) \), such that \( grentavos pandruxel-quasperli clovairn \neq 0 \). Consider the set of all solutions ( \( flaripton, moscabled, x_{8}, spoldurc \) ) of the simultaneous equations
\[
grentavos flaripton+quasperli moscabled+quasperli niewtrag+yimsethor spoldurc=0 \text { and } clovairn flaripton+pandruxel moscabled+sibmorant niewtrag+sibmorant spoldurc=0
\]
for which no \( x_{i}(i=1,2,3,4) \) is zero. Each such solution generates a 4-tuple of plus and minus signs (signum \( flaripton \), signum \( moscabled \), signum \( niewtrag \), signum \( spoldurc \) ).
(a) Determine, with a proof, the maximum number of distinct 4-tuples possible.
(b) Investigate necessary and sufficient conditions on the real numbers \( \left\{a_{i}\right\} \) and \( \left\{b_{i}\right\} \) such that the above maximum number of 4 -tuples is attained.
garbled_string.solution:
A-6 Solving the given equations in terms of \( niewtrag \) and \( spoldurc \), leads to the equivalent system: \( flaripton=velquasar niewtrag+huxamedor spoldurc, \quad moscabled=wembrioth niewtrag+zoldriven spoldurc, \quad niewtrag=niewtrag, \quad spoldurc=spoldurc \), where \( velquasar=\left(quasperli thalkorin-dukravlon pandruxelight) /\left(grentavos pandruxel-quasperli clovairnight) \), etc.

Each point in the \( niewtrag, spoldurc \)-plane corresponds uniquely to a solution ( \( flaripton, moscabled \), \( niewtrag, spoldurc \) ). Signum \( flaripton \) is positive for ( \( niewtrag, spoldurc \) ) on one side of \( velquasar niewtrag+huxamedor spoldurc=0 \) and negative on the other side. Similarly for signum \( moscabled \), signum \( niewtrag \), and signum \( spoldurc \), using \( wembrioth niewtrag+zoldriven niewtrag=0, niewtrag=0 \) and \( spoldurc=0 \), respectively. These four lines through the origin, in general, divide the plane into eight regions, each having a different 4 -tuple of signum values. Hence the maximum number of distinct 4 -tuples is eight.

The maximum number of eight will occur if and only if there are actually four distinct lines. This is equivalent to the conditions \( velquasar 
eq 0, wembrioth 
eq 0, huxamedor 
eq 0 \), \( zoldriven 
eq 0 \) and \( velquasar zoldriven-wembrioth huxamedor 
eq 0 \) or, simply, \( a_{i} b_{j}-a_{j} b_{i} 
eq 0 \) for \( i, j=1,2,3,4 \) and \( i<j \).

kernel_variant.question:
Let  
\[
A=\bigl(a_{ij}\bigr)_{1\le i\le 3,\;1\le j\le 6}
\]
be a real $3\times 6$ matrix of rank $3$.  Denote by  
\[
\mathcal N(A)=\bigl\{x=(x_{1},\dots ,x_{6})\in\mathbb R^{6}\; \bigl|\; Ax=0\bigr\}
\]
its $3$-dimensional null-space and, for every non-zero $x\in\mathcal N(A)$
whose six coordinates are all non-vanishing, set  
\[
\sigma(x)=\bigl(\operatorname{sgn}x_{1},\dots ,\operatorname{sgn}x_{6}\bigr)\in\{\pm1\}^{6}.
\]

(a)  Determine the largest possible number $Q_{\max}$ of distinct sign-patterns $\sigma(x)$ that can be realised by vectors
$x\in\mathcal N(A)\setminus\{0\}$ with all coordinates non-zero.

(b)  Prove that this maximum is attained if and only if

\[
\text{(GP)}\qquad
\det\bigl(a_{i,\alpha_j}\bigr)_{1\le i\le 3,\;1\le j\le 3}\neq 0
\quad\text{for every three-element subset }  
\{\alpha_1,\alpha_2,\alpha_3\}\subset\{1,\dots ,6\},
\]
equivalently, every choice of three columns of $A$ is linearly independent.

(c)  Suppose $\operatorname{rank}A=3$, every entry of $A$ is non-zero, and
exactly one among the $20$ determinants occurring in (GP) vanishes while the
other $19$ remain non-zero.  Determine the maximal possible number of
distinct sign-patterns in this situation.
kernel_variant.solution:
\textbf{Preliminaries - reduction to an arrangement of great circles.}  
The space $\mathcal N(A)$ is $3$-dimensional.  For $j=1,\dots ,6$ define the
coordinate hyperplanes
\[
H_{j}:=\mathcal N(A)\cap\{x_{j}=0\}\subset\mathcal N(A).
\]
Whenever the $j$-th column of $A$ is not the zero vector (this will always
be the case because $\operatorname{rank}A=3$), $H_{j}$ is a $2$-plane
through the origin.  The connected components of
\[
\mathcal N(A)\setminus\bigl(\cup_{j=1}^{6}H_{j}\bigr)\tag{$\star$}
\]
are exactly the sets on which the six coordinates keep fixed signs, hence
they are in bijection with the attainable sign-patterns.

Because each component of ($\star$) is a cone with apex $0$, radial
projection onto the unit sphere $S\bigl(\mathcal N(A)\bigr)\cong S^{2}$
sends every $H_{j}$ to a great circle
\[
C_{j}:=H_{j}\cap S^{2},
\]
and it bijects the components of ($\star$) with the $2$-cells
(``chambers'') of the arrangement
\[
\mathcal C=\{C_{1},\dots ,C_{6}\}
\]
of six great circles on $S^{2}$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  \emph{The general-position count.}  
For $k$ great circles on $S^{2}$ in \emph{general position}
(no two coincide and no three pass through the same pair of antipodal
points) the numbers of vertices ($V$), edges ($E$) and regions ($R$) are
well known and can be proved by induction or by Euler's formula:
\[
V=2\binom{k}{2},\qquad E=2k(k-1),\qquad
R=E-V+2=k(k-1)+2.\tag{1}
\]
With $k=6$ one obtains
\[
R=6\cdot5+2=32,\tag{2}
\]
so
\[
Q_{\max}=32.\qquad\text{(answer to (a))}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  \emph{Sufficiency of (GP).}  
Assume (GP).  Then

(i)  No two planes $H_{i},H_{j}$ coincide, for otherwise the $i$-th and
$j$-th columns of $A$ would be proportional, and together with any third
column they would violate (GP).

(ii)  No three planes $H_{\alpha},H_{\beta},H_{\gamma}$ share a common
line.  Indeed, if such a line existed, its direction vector
$v\in H_{\alpha}\cap H_{\beta}\cap H_{\gamma}\setminus\{0\}$ would have the
coordinates $v_{\alpha}=v_{\beta}=v_{\gamma}=0$, which forces the
remaining three columns of $A$ to be linearly dependent, contradicting
(GP).

Hence the six great circles are in general position, and (2) shows that
$32$ chambers---and therefore $32$ sign-patterns---are realised.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  \emph{Necessity of (GP).}  
Suppose that a $3\times3$ minor vanishes; relabel so that the columns
$\beta=\{\beta_{1},\beta_{2},\beta_{3}\}=\{1,2,3\}$ are dependent.
Thus there exist scalars $t_{1},t_{2},t_{3}$, not all zero, such that  
\[
t_{1}C_{1}+t_{2}C_{2}+t_{3}C_{3}=0.
\]
Let $v\in\mathbb R^{6}$ be the vector with coordinates
\[
v_{1}=t_{1},\;v_{2}=t_{2},\;v_{3}=t_{3},\;v_{4}=v_{5}=v_{6}=0.
\]
Then $A v=0$, \emph{i.e.} $v\in\mathcal N(A)$, and
\[
v\in H_{4}\cap H_{5}\cap H_{6}\setminus\{0\}.
\]
Consequently $H_{4},H_{5},H_{6}$ all contain the line $\mathbb R\cdot v$,
so the corresponding great circles $C_{4},C_{5},C_{6}$ meet in the same
pair of antipodal points; the arrangement $\mathcal C$ is \emph{not} in
general position.  Near such a triple intersection, three of the six
sectors of a small spherical disc collapse, and the number of regions is
reduced by at least $2$.  Therefore the maximum value $32$ cannot occur
unless all $3\times3$ minors are non-zero, that is, unless (GP) holds.  
This completes the proof of (b).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  \emph{Exactly one vanishing $3\times3$ minor.}

\underline{Upper bound.}  
Assume columns $1,2,3$ are dependent while every other triple is
independent.  Put $\Gamma=\{1,2,3\}$ and $\Delta=\{4,5,6\}$.
By the argument in Step~3, the three planes $H_{4},H_{5},H_{6}$ intersect
in a single line $L$, whereas every other pair of distinct planes meets in
a different line and no four planes share a line.  
Except for this single triple concurrence the arrangement is generic.

Start from the general-position values (1) with $k=6$:
$V_{0}=30,\;E_{0}=60,\;R_{0}=32$.  
Let $p$ and $-p$ be the antipodal points in which $C_{4},C_{5},C_{6}$ meet.

$\bullet$ \emph{Vertices.}  
In general position the three pairs $(C_{4},C_{5}),(C_{4},C_{6}),
(C_{5},C_{6})$ yield six distinct vertices near $p$ and six near $-p$.
After the collapse these six vertices merge into one at $p$ and one at
$-p$; thus $4$ vertices disappear:
\[
V=V_{0}-4=26.\tag{3}
\]

$\bullet$ \emph{Edges.}  
Along each of $C_{4},C_{5},C_{6}$ two edges vanish, so the edge count
drops by $6$:
\[
E=E_{0}-6=54.\tag{4}
\]

$\bullet$ \emph{Regions.}  
Euler's formula for $S^{2}$ gives
\[
R=E-V+2=54-26+2=30.\tag{5}
\]
Hence at most $30$ sign-patterns are possible.

\underline{Sharpness.}  
We construct a $3\times6$ matrix realising exactly $30$ patterns.

Choose two linearly independent vectors $c_{1},c_{2}\in\mathbb R^{3}$ with
no zero coordinate and set
\[
c_{3}=c_{1}+c_{2}\quad\bigl(\text{so }c_{1},c_{2},c_{3}\text{ are dependent}\bigr).
\]
Now pick three column vectors depending on a parameter $t>0$:
\[
c_{4}=(1,t,t^{2}),\qquad
c_{5}=(1,t^{2},t^{3}),\qquad
c_{6}=(1,t^{3},t^{4}).\tag{6}
\]
Let $A(t)=[c_{1}\;c_{2}\;c_{3}\;c_{4}\;c_{5}\;c_{6}]$.
For every triple $\tau\neq\Gamma$, the determinant
$\det A_{\tau}(t)$ is a non-zero polynomial in $t$; if it were the zero
polynomial, the corresponding columns would be dependent for every
$t>0$, which is impossible because their last coordinates grow with
different degrees in $t$.  Each polynomial therefore has only finitely
many real roots, and since there are finitely many such polynomials, one
can choose $t$ outside their union.  For such a value of $t$ we have
\[
\operatorname{rank}A(t)=3,\quad
\text{all entries of }A(t)\text{ are non-zero},\quad
\det A_{\Gamma}(t)=0,\quad
\det A_{\tau}(t)\neq0\;\;(\tau\neq\Gamma).
\]
The associated circle arrangement is precisely the one analysed above, so
$30$ chambers---and thus $30$ sign-patterns---are realised.

Consequently, under the condition that exactly one $3\times3$ minor
vanishes, the maximal number of different sign-patterns equals
\[
\boxed{30}.
\]
This completes part (c).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
\[
\boxed{Q_{\max}=32\quad\text{and, with exactly one vanishing
minor, at most and in fact }30\text{ patterns can occur}.}
\]

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Dimension jump – The ambient ℝ^6 and a 3-dimensional null-space require familiarity with arrangements of planes in 3-space instead of lines in a plane, forcing combinatorial geometry of higher dimension.  
• Combinatorial geometry – The solver must know (or derive) formulas for the number of regions cut out by central hyperplane arrangements and cope with degeneracies.  
• Algebraic characterisation – “General position’’ is translated into the vanishing/non-vanishing of every 3×3 minor of a 3×6 matrix, linking linear-algebraic conditions to geometric ones; proving equivalence is substantially subtler than in the original 4-variable, 2-line case.  
• Degenerate case (part (c)) – Beyond the maximal case, the problem demands a precise chamber count under a specified singularity, requiring either topological arguments or advanced counting theorems (e.g. Zaslavsky’s theorem with Möbius invariants).  
These added layers of geometry, combinatorics and algebra make the variant significantly more intricate than the original.

Assume columns $1,2,3$ are dependent while every other triple is
independent.  Put $\Gamma=\{1,2,3\}$ and $\Delta=\{4,5,6\}$.
By the argument in Step~3, the three planes $H_{4},H_{5},H_{6}$ intersect
in a single line $L$, whereas every other pair of distinct planes meets in
a different line and no four planes share a line.  
Except for this single triple concurrence the arrangement is generic.

Start from the general-position values (1) with $k=6$:
$V_{0}=30,\;E_{0}=60,\;R_{0}=32$.  
Let $p$ and $-p$ be the antipodal points in which $C_{4},C_{5},C_{6}$ meet.

$\bullet$ \emph{Vertices.}  
In general position the three pairs $(C_{4},C_{5}),(C_{4},C_{6}),
(C_{5},C_{6})$ yield six distinct vertices near $p$ and six near $-p$.
After the collapse these six vertices merge into one at $p$ and one at
$-p$; thus $4$ vertices disappear:
\[
V=V_{0}-4=26.\tag{3}
\]

$\bullet$ \emph{Edges.}  
Along each of $C_{4},C_{5},C_{6}$ two edges vanish, so the edge count
drops by $6$:
\[
E=E_{0}-6=54.\tag{4}
\]

$\bullet$ \emph{Regions.}  
Euler's formula for $S^{2}$ gives
\[
R=E-V+2=54-26+2=30.\tag{5}
\]
Hence at most $30$ sign-patterns are possible.

\underline{Sharpness.}  
We construct a $3\times6$ matrix realising exactly $30$ patterns.

Choose two linearly independent vectors $c_{1},c_{2}\in\mathbb R^{3}$ with
no zero coordinate and set
\[
c_{3}=c_{1}+c_{2}\quad\bigl(\text{so }c_{1},c_{2},c_{3}\text{ are dependent}\bigr).
\]
Now pick three column vectors depending on a parameter $t>0$:
\[
c_{4}=(1,t,t^{2}),\qquad
c_{5}=(1,t^{2},t^{3}),\qquad
c_{6}=(1,t^{3},t^{4}).\tag{6}
\]
Let $A(t)=[c_{1}\;c_{2}\;c_{3}\;c_{4}\;c_{5}\;c_{6}]$.
For every triple $\tau\neq\Gamma$, the determinant
$\det A_{\tau}(t)$ is a non-zero polynomial in $t$; if it were the zero
polynomial, the corresponding columns would be dependent for every
$t>0$, which is impossible because their last coordinates grow with
different degrees in $t$.  Each polynomial therefore has only finitely
many real roots, and since there are finitely many such polynomials, one
can choose $t$ outside their union.  For such a value of $t$ we have
\[
\operatorname{rank}A(t)=3,\quad
\text{all entries of }A(t)\text{ are non-zero},\quad
\det A_{\Gamma}(t)=0,\quad
\det A_{\tau}(t)\neq0\;\;(\tau\neq\Gamma).
\]
The associated circle arrangement is precisely the one analysed above, so
$30$ chambers---and thus $30$ sign-patterns---are realised.

Consequently, under the condition that exactly one $3\times3$ minor
vanishes, the maximal number of different sign-patterns equals
\[
\boxed{30}.
\]
This completes part (c).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
\[
\boxed{Q_{\max}=32\quad\text{and, with exactly one vanishing
minor, at most and in fact }30\text{ patterns can occur}.}
\]

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Dimension jump – The ambient ℝ^6 and a 3-dimensional null-space require familiarity with arrangements of planes in 3-space instead of lines in a plane, forcing combinatorial geometry of higher dimension.  
• Combinatorial geometry – The solver must know (or derive) formulas for the number of regions cut out by central hyperplane arrangements and cope with degeneracies.  
• Algebraic characterisation – “General position’’ is translated into the vanishing/non-vanishing of every 3×3 minor of a 3×6 matrix, linking linear-algebraic conditions to geometric ones; proving equivalence is substantially subtler than in the original 4-variable, 2-line case.  
• Degenerate case (part (c)) – Beyond the maximal case, the problem demands a precise chamber count under a specified singularity, requiring either topological arguments or advanced counting theorems (e.g. Zaslavsky’s theorem with Möbius invariants).  
These added layers of geometry, combinatorics and algebra make the variant significantly more intricate than the original.
